1.synthesis: Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, returns from the Jordan and is led by the Spirit in the wilderness for forty days, tempted by the devil (vv.1–2). Three specific temptations follow: (1) turn stone to bread when hungry — Jesus cites Deuteronomy 8:3 (vv.3–4); (2) receive all the kingdoms shown in an instant, if he will worship the devil — Jesus cites Deuteronomy 6:13 (vv.5–8); (3) throw himself from the Temple pinnacle since the angels will catch him — the devil cites Psalm 91:11–12; Jesus cites Deuteronomy 6:16 (vv.9–12). The devil departs "until an opportune time" (v.13). Luke's order of the second and third temptations is reversed from Matthew's — ending with the Temple in Jerusalem, the city that will be the narrative destination of Luke's entire Gospel.
1.historical_context: The forty-day wilderness period echoes Israel's forty-year wilderness wandering (Deuteronomy 8:2) and Moses's forty days on the mountain (Exodus 34:28). All three of Jesus's responses come from Deuteronomy — specifically from the wilderness discourse sections (Deut 6 and 8) that address Israel's temptation and failure in the desert. The devil's offer of "all the kingdoms of the world" (v.5) — "in a moment of time," a phrase unique to Luke — reflects the Jewish background of the evil one as controller of the present age's political powers (cf. John 12:31; 2 Corinthians 4:4). The Temple pinnacle was the high point of the Temple complex; a drop from there into the Kidron Valley below would be fatal. Luke's note that the devil departed "until an opportune time" (v.13) anticipates the return of Satan at the Passion: 22:3 ("then Satan entered Judas") is the moment Luke signals as that opportune time.
1.literary_structure: Luke's distinctive reversal of Matthew's second and third temptations gives Luke's account a Jerusalem focus: the final temptation is at the Temple, and the devil's departure "until an opportune time" creates a narrative bracket with the Passion. The three temptations move from personal need (bread) to universal dominion (kingdoms) to religious presumption (Temple pinnacle) — an escalation from physical to political to spiritual claims. Each temptation tests a different dimension of the Son's identity declared at the baptism: will he use divine power for self-provision? Will he receive kingdom-authority through illegitimate means? Will he presume on divine protection? The answer to each is a Deuteronomy citation — Jesus fights the devil with the word that Israel failed to live by in their own wilderness.
1.theological_application: The temptation narrative is the testing of the new Adam in the wilderness that the first Adam failed in the garden. Where Adam ate what was forbidden in circumstances of abundance, Jesus refuses to eat when he is starving, trusting that "man shall not live by bread alone" (v.4). Where Israel grumbled for what God had not yet given, Jesus declines illegitimate possession. The categories of 1 John 2:16 — "the lust of the flesh, the lust of the eyes, the pride of life" — map onto the three temptations: bread (flesh), kingdoms shown (eyes), Temple pinnacle presumption (pride). Jesus demonstrates the obedience of the Son that opens the way of redemption for those who have failed as Adam and Israel failed.
1.christology: "If you are the Son of God..." (v.3, v.9). The devil's two conditional taunts target precisely the identity declared at the baptism — the devil's strategy is to get Jesus to prove, demonstrate, or claim his sonship on the devil's terms. Jesus refuses. He will not use the Father's power for personal provision; he will not receive the Father's kingdom through the father of lies; he will not test the Father's faithfulness by manufactured crisis. Sonship, for Jesus, means trusting the Father's timing, receiving the Father's authority, and relying on the Father's care — not coercing any of these through divine display. This is the shape of the obedience of the Son that the cross will complete.

14.synthesis: Jesus returns to Galilee in the power of the Spirit, teaching in synagogues and being glorified by all (vv.14–15). He goes to Nazareth, enters the synagogue on the Sabbath, stands to read, and is handed the Isaiah scroll. He reads Isaiah 61:1–2: "The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor..." — and stops before Isaiah's phrase "and the day of vengeance of our God" (vv.16–19). He sits down and says: "Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing" (vv.20–21). At first all speak well of him and marvel. But when he presses the point — "no prophet is accepted in his hometown" — and cites Elijah sent to the widow of Zarephath and Elisha healing Naaman the Syrian rather than any in Israel, the synagogue erupts in rage; they drive him out and lead him to a cliff to throw him off, but he passes through their midst and goes away (vv.22–30).
14.historical_context: Luke places the Nazareth rejection at the very beginning of Jesus's Galilean ministry (before Mark's version in ch. 6 after extensive ministry), making it a programmatic scene for the entire Gospel. The scroll of Isaiah read in the synagogue — on which Jesus finds the relevant passage — was the same Isaiah whose prophecy John had cited (ch. 3). Isaiah 61:1–2 was read messianically in the Qumran community (11QMelchizedek applies it to the eschatological year of jubilee). The deliberate truncation of the Isaiah citation before "the day of vengeance" is theologically significant: Jesus's first coming is characterized by the year of the Lord's favor; the judgment belongs to a future reckoning. The Elijah/Elisha examples are pointed: both great Israelite prophets had been sent in their ministry to non-Israelites (the Phoenician widow of Zarephath, 1 Kings 17; the Syrian general Naaman, 2 Kings 5), bypassing the needy of Israel.
14.literary_structure: The Nazareth scene is Luke's programmatic pericope — the manifesto of Jesus's mission stated in his own scriptural self-interpretation. The movement from initial acceptance ("all spoke well of him," v.22) to attempted murder (v.29) prefigures the arc of the entire Gospel: the ministry that begins in acclaim ends at Golgotha. The "today" (sēmeron) of v.21 is a characteristic Lukan word: salvation is a present-tense event in Luke (cf. 2:11; 19:9; 23:43). The crowd's question "Is this not Joseph's son?" (v.22) sets up the scandal: the hometown boy claiming to fulfill Isaiah 61 in their hearing is either deluded or divine.
14.theological_application: The mission statement of Isaiah 61:1–2 — "good news to the poor, release to the captives, recovering of sight to the blind, liberty to the oppressed, the year of the Lord's favor" — has defined Christian social ethics across two millennia. The "poor" (ptōchoi) in Luke consistently carries both material and spiritual dimensions; the Gospel of Luke will demonstrate Jesus's ministry to the literally poor, the social outcasts, and the spiritually broken with equal consistency. The year of the Lord's favor echoes the Jubilee (Leviticus 25) — the sabbatical reset of debts, freedom, and land — now proclaimed as an eschatological reality arriving in Jesus's person and ministry.
14.christology: "Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing" (v.21). Jesus does not say "this Scripture points to a future figure" or "I will fulfill this someday" but "fulfilled today" — as he speaks, the proclamation IS the fulfillment. The Spirit-anointing of Isaiah 61:1 ("the Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me") identifies Jesus as the one the Spirit descended upon at the Jordan: the baptism was the anointing the prophet anticipated. That he stops before "the day of vengeance" (Isaiah 61:2) shows christological discernment about the two comings: the first coming is the year of favor; the second coming will include the reckoning that today's reading deliberately omits.

31.synthesis: Jesus goes down to Capernaum and teaches in the synagogue on the Sabbath. The people are astonished at his teaching, for his word is with authority (vv.31–32). A man with an unclean spirit cries out: "Ha! What do you want with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are — the Holy One of God!" (vv.33–34). Jesus rebukes the demon: "Be silent and come out of him!" The demon throws the man down without hurting him and comes out (v.35). Amazement comes over the people: they say to one another, "What is this word? For with authority and power he commands the unclean spirits, and they come out!" Reports spread throughout the surrounding region (vv.36–37). The exorcism in the Capernaum synagogue is Jesus's first authoritative act in Luke after the programmatic Nazareth sermon.
31.historical_context: Capernaum (Kfar Nahum, "village of Nahum") on the north shore of the Sea of Galilee was a significant fishing and trading center and the base of Jesus's Galilean ministry. Synagogue exorcisms were not unheard of in the Jewish world — rabbinic literature mentions various apotropaic practices — but Jesus's method (a direct verbal command without formula, name-invocation, or ritual) was markedly different from the exorcistic traditions described by Josephus (Ant. 8.2.5) and documented in magical papyri. The demon's identification of Jesus as "the Holy One of God" (ho hagios tou theou) is a messianic title, likely drawing on Psalm 16:10 and Numbers 16:7; it is also the language of Aaron as the holy one of God (Psalm 106:16). The demon recognizes what the crowd is only beginning to perceive.
31.literary_structure: The Capernaum exorcism follows the Nazareth rejection structurally: Jesus is rejected in his hometown but is recognized (by a demon) and received (with amazement) in Capernaum. The paradox — rejection by those who know him most, recognition by those who know him least — will be a recurring Lukan theme. The crowd's double response of astonishment (v.32 at the teaching, v.36 at the exorcism) establishes the connection: the same authority that characterizes Jesus's word characterizes his acts. "His word" (ho logos autou) is the subject of both sentences — the word that astonishes in the synagogue is the same word that commands the demon to leave.
31.theological_application: The demon's demand, "Have you come to destroy us?" (v.34), is the clearest statement in the exorcism narratives of what Jesus's ministry means from the perspective of the opposing powers: it is destruction of their domain. The messianic mission announced at Nazareth — release to the captives, liberty to the oppressed — is here enacted in the most concrete form: a human being held captive by a spiritual oppressor is set free by a single authoritative word. The kingdom of God is not merely proclaimed in Luke; it confronts and displaces the contrary kingdom wherever the word of Jesus is spoken.
31.christology: "I know who you are — the Holy One of God!" (v.34). The demon's confession is the clearest christological statement thus far in Luke's narrative — made, paradoxically, by the opposition. The messianic secret (not publishing one's identity) is preserved when Jesus silences the demon (v.35); but the reader has heard the confession. The divine authority that the Spirit descending at the Jordan had anointed and that the Deuteronomy citations in the wilderness had protected is now in direct confrontation with the demonic order — and its superiority is immediate and total. The one who is "full of the Holy Spirit" (4:1) drives out the unclean spirit with the word of that same Spirit.

38.synthesis: Leaving the synagogue, Jesus enters the house of Simon (Peter); Simon's mother-in-law is gripped by a high fever and they ask Jesus about her. He stands over her and rebukes the fever, and it leaves her immediately — she rises and serves them (vv.38–39). At sunset, all who have any sick with various diseases bring them to Jesus, and he lays his hands on each one and heals them (v.40). Demons come out of many, crying, "You are the Son of God!" — but he rebukes them and will not allow them to speak, because they know he is the Christ (v.41). At daybreak he departs to a desolate place, and the crowds seek him. They try to keep him from leaving but he says: "I must preach the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns as well; for I was sent for this purpose" (vv.42–44). He preaches in the synagogues of Judea.
38.historical_context: Luke's detail that Jesus "lays his hands on each one" (v.40) — rather than Mark's briefer description of healing — reflects Luke's medical and pastoral attentiveness (he is, per Colossians 4:14, "the beloved physician"). Peter's home in Capernaum was identified by a second-century tradition preserved in the graffiti of the octagonal church built over it. The sunset timing (v.40) is significant: sunset ended the Sabbath, when Jewish law would have restricted carrying the sick through the streets — the crowd waited until they could move freely. The demons' confession "You are the Son of God" (v.41) is more developed than the Capernaum exorcism's "Holy One of God," and Luke explicitly glosses it: they know he is the Christ. The departure to a desolate place for prayer (v.42, implied though not stated explicitly as in Mark 1:35) and the mission-statement about other towns frames Jesus's healings within a larger preaching purpose.
38.literary_structure: The evening of healings concludes the first full Sabbath-to-evening sequence in Luke's Galilean ministry: synagogue teaching (vv.31–32) → synagogue exorcism (vv.33–37) → Peter's house healing (vv.38–39) → mass healing at sunset (vv.40–41) → morning withdrawal and mission statement (vv.42–44). The day-long arc of Jesus's ministry shows the interpenetration of teaching, exorcism, and healing as a unified whole, each flowing from the same authoritative word. The mission statement in v.43 — "I must (dei) preach... for I was sent (apestalen) for this purpose" — frames everything as obedience to the Father's sending purpose, not spontaneous responses to need.
38.theological_application: "I must preach the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns as well; for I was sent for this purpose" (v.43). The divine dei ("must," "it is necessary") is one of Luke's most important theological words: it frames Jesus's entire ministry as divinely necessary and divinely directed. He does not stay in Capernaum to build a healing center, though the crowds want him to ("they tried to keep him from leaving"); he moves because he is sent to move. The kingdom of God is his message, and the healings and exorcisms are its signs — demonstrations that the rule of God is arriving, displacing sickness and oppression wherever Jesus goes. Ministry shaped by the Father's sending purpose rather than by the crowd's demand is the shape of faithful service.
38.christology: "They know he is the Christ" (v.41). Luke's explanatory gloss on the demons' confession links «Son of God» with «Christ» — the anointed one, the Messiah. The Spirit who descended at the Jordan, the Spirit who led him through the wilderness, the Spirit the Isaiah reading named as resting upon him — is the same Spirit whose power commands the demons and heals the sick. Jesus's first day of ministry in Capernaum is a compressed demonstration of the Isaiah 61 program: good news preached (teaching), captives released (exorcisms), oppressed freed (healings). The same one who said "today this scripture is fulfilled" is now fulfilling it in a village-by-village progress through Galilee.
